examples.explanation:
Start: lcs(0,0)
  s1[0]='A', s2[0]='A' → MATCH
  Call: 1 + lcs(1,1)
    lcs(1,1): s1[1]='B', s2[1]='C' → MISMATCH
    Branch 1: lcs(2,1) → s1[2]='C', s2[1]='C' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(3,2) → base case → 1
    Branch 2: lcs(1,2) → base case (j=2 ≥ len(s2)) → 0
    max(1,0) = 1
  Result: 1 + 1 = 2
Final: 2

Start: lcs(0,0)
  s1[0]='B', s2[0]='A' → MISMATCH
  Branch 1: lcs(1,0) → s1[1]='D', s2[0]='A' → MISMATCH → ... (complex tree)
  Branch 2: lcs(0,1) → s1[0]='B', s2[1]='B' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(1,2)
    lcs(1,2): s1[1]='D', s2[2]='C' → MISMATCH
    Branch 2.1: lcs(2,2) → s1[2]='C', s2[2]='C' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(3,3)
      lcs(3,3): s1[3]='A', s2[3]='B' → MISMATCH
      Branch 2.1.1: lcs(4,3) → s1[4]='B', s2[3]='B' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(5,4) → base case → 1
      Branch 2.1.2: lcs(3,4) → s1[3]='A', s2[4]='D' → MISMATCH → ...
      max(1, ...) = 1
    Result: 1 + 1 = 2
    Branch 2.2: lcs(1,3) → ...
    max(2, ...) = 2
  Result: 1 + 2 = 3
  Continue exploring other branches...
  Maximum found: 4 (sequence: B, C, A, B or B, D, A, B)
Final: 4

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsequences of both strings by comparing characters at each index, branching on match or mismatch. Use it for understanding the problem but not for practical solutions due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Longest Common Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsequence combinations of two strings.

examples.trace:
Start: lcs(0,0)
  s1[0]='A', s2[0]='A' → MATCH
  Call: 1 + lcs(1,1)
    lcs(1,1): s1[1]='B', s2[1]='C' → MISMATCH
    Branch 1: lcs(2,1) → s1[2]='C', s2[1]='C' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(3,2) → base case → 1
    Branch 2: lcs(1,2) → base case (j=2 ≥ len(s2)) → 0
    max(1,0) = 1
  Result: 1 + 1 = 2
Final: 2

Start: lcs(0,0)
  s1[0]='B', s2[0]='A' → MISMATCH
  Branch 1: lcs(1,0) → s1[1]='D', s2[0]='A' → MISMATCH → ... (complex tree)
  Branch 2: lcs(0,1) → s1[0]='B', s2[1]='B' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(1,2)
    lcs(1,2): s1[1]='D', s2[2]='C' → MISMATCH
    Branch 2.1: lcs(2,2) → s1[2]='C', s2[2]='C' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(3,3)
      lcs(3,3): s1[3]='A', s2[3]='B' → MISMATCH
      Branch 2.1.1: lcs(4,3) → s1[4]='B', s2[3]='B' → MATCH → 1 + lcs(5,4) → base case → 1
      Branch 2.1.2: lcs(3,4) → s1[3]='A', s2[4]='D' → MISMATCH → ...
      max(1, ...) = 1
    Result: 1 + 1 = 2
    Branch 2.2: lcs(1,3) → ...
    max(2, ...) = 2
  Result: 1 + 2 = 3
  Continue exploring other branches...
  Maximum found: 4 (sequence: B, C, A, B or B, D, A, B)
Final: 4

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Character Matching with Branching
ladders.desc: The recursive function that compares characters at given indices and branches on match/mismatch.

examples.trace:
Call: lcs_helper("AB", "AC", 0, 0)
  s1[0]='A', s2[0]='A' → MATCH
  Return: 1 + lcs_helper("AB", "AC", 1, 1)
    lcs_helper("AB", "AC", 1, 1):
      s1[1]='B', s2[1]='C' → MISMATCH
      Branch 1: lcs_helper("AB", "AC", 2, 1) → base case (i=2) → 0
      Branch 2: lcs_helper("AB", "AC", 1, 2) → base case (j=2) → 0
      max(0,0) = 0
    Result: 1 + 0 = 1
Final: 1

Call: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 1, 1)
  s1[1]='B', s2[1]='X' → MISMATCH
  Branch 1: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 2, 1)
    s1[2]='C', s2[1]='X' → MISMATCH
    Branch 1.1: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 3, 1) → base case → 0
    Branch 1.2: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 2, 2) → s1[2]='C', s2[2]='C' → MATCH → 1 + lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 3, 3) → base case → 1
    max(0,1) = 1
  Branch 2: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 1, 2)
    s1[1]='B', s2[2]='C' → MISMATCH
    Branch 2.1: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 2, 2) → as above → 1
    Branch 2.2: lcs_helper("ABC", "AXC", 1, 3) → base case → 0
    max(1,0) = 1
  max(1,1) = 1
Final: 1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching on Character Match/Mismatch
ladders.desc: The decision logic: when characters match, include and advance both; when mismatch, explore both skip options.

examples.trace:
Position: i=0, j=0
Characters: s1[0]='A', s2[0]='A'
Comparison: 'A' == 'A' → MATCH
Action: Include 'A' in LCS (add 1 to count)
Move: i → 1, j → 1
Next: Solve subproblem from (1,1)
Result: 1 + [result from (1,1)]

Position: i=0, j=0
Characters: s1[0]='A', s2[0]='C'
Comparison: 'A' != 'C' → MISMATCH
Action: Explore two branches:
Branch 1: Skip s1[0] → solve from (1,0)
  s1[1]='B', s2[0]='C' → 'B' != 'C' → mismatch
  Further branching...
Branch 2: Skip s2[0] → solve from (0,1)
  s1[0]='A', s2[1]='D' → 'A' != 'D' → mismatch
  Further branching...
Take maximum of both branch results

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Subsequence Generation
ladders.desc: Generating all subsequences of a string using recursion.
ladders.explanation:
This building block is essential for understanding how the brute force approach explores all possibilities. A subsequence is any sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more characters without changing order. To generate all subsequences recursively, we use a decision tree: at each character position, we have two choices - include the character in the current subsequence or exclude it. The intuition is that we can build subsequences incrementally: starting from an empty subsequence, for each character, we branch into two paths: one where we add the character, and one where we skip it. This generates 2^n possible subsequences for a string of length n. In LCS, we need to compare all subsequences of s1 against all subsequences of s2, which is why we get O(2^(n+m)) complexity. This connects to L2 because the match/mismatch logic is essentially making these include/exclude decisions based on character equality. Time complexity is O(2^n) to generate all subsequences. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Common mistakes: confusing subsequences with substrings (subsequences don't need to be contiguous), not understanding the binary decision tree, or incorrectly implementing the recursion termination.

Algorithm:
1. Start with empty current subsequence, index i=0
2. Base case: if i == n, record/store current subsequence
3. Recursive case:
   - Branch 1: Exclude s[i] → recurse with i+1, same subsequence
   - Branch 2: Include s[i] → recurse with i+1, subsequence + s[i]

Pseudocode:
def generate_subseq(s, i=0, current=""):
    if i == len(s):
        # Base case: reached end of string
        print(current)  # or add to list
        return
    
    # Branch 1: exclude current character
    generate_subseq(s, i+1, current)
    
    # Branch 2: include current character
    generate_subseq(s, i+1, current + s[i])

examples.trace:
Start: generate_subseq("AB", 0, "")
  i=0, current=""
  Branch 1 (exclude 'A'): generate_subseq("AB", 1, "")
    i=1, current=""
    Branch 1.1 (exclude 'B'): generate_subseq("AB", 2, "") → base case → print ""
    Branch 1.2 (include 'B'): generate_subseq("AB", 2, "B") → base case → print "B"
  Branch 2 (include 'A'): generate_subseq("AB", 1, "A")
    i=1, current="A"
    Branch 2.1 (exclude 'B'): generate_subseq("AB", 2, "A") → base case → print "A"
    Branch 2.2 (include 'B'): generate_subseq("AB", 2, "AB") → base case → print "AB"
All subsequences: "", "B", "A", "AB"

Start: generate_subseq("A", 0, "")
  i=0, current=""
  Branch 1 (exclude 'A'): generate_subseq("A", 1, "") → base case → print ""
  Branch 2 (include 'A'): generate_subseq("A", 1, "A") → base case → print "A"
All subsequences: "", "A"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and Index Advancement
ladders.desc: Comparing characters at specific indices and moving to next positions.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check bounds: i=0 < 3, j=0 < 2 ✓
Step 2: Access characters: s1[0]='A', s2[0]='A'
Step 3: Compare: 'A' == 'A' → match
Step 4: Action: advance both indices
Result: new indices i=1, j=1

examples.output: mismatch, could advance i to (2,1) OR j to (1,2)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Check bounds: i=1 < 3, j=1 < 3 ✓
Step 2: Access characters: s1[1]='B', s2[1]='E'
Step 3: Compare: 'B' != 'E' → mismatch
Step 4: Possible actions:
  Option A: advance i only → new indices (2,1)
  Option B: advance j only → new indices (1,2)
Both options must be explored in brute force

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings, Indices, and Character Access
ladders.desc: Basic string operations: accessing characters by index and understanding string length.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept covers the most basic operations needed for LCS: understanding strings as sequences of characters, accessing individual characters using indices (positions), and determining string length. A string is an ordered collection of characters where each character has a position starting from 0 (zero-based indexing). The intuition is that to process a string algorithmically, we need to be able to examine each character in order. Character access s[i] retrieves the character at position i. String length len(s) tells us how many characters are in the string. These operations are O(1) time complexity. This connects to L4 because comparing characters requires accessing them first, and advancing indices requires understanding positions within strings. Common beginner mistakes: confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, accessing indices beyond string length (causing index errors), not understanding that strings are immutable in many languages, or confusing characters with strings of length 1. The key insight is that all string algorithms build on these basic operations of traversal and access.

Basic operations:
1. String declaration: s = "ABC"
2. Length: n = len(s)  # n = 3
3. Character access: s[0] = 'A', s[1] = 'B', s[2] = 'C'
4. Index range: valid indices are 0 to len(s)-1

Pseudocode:
# Example string operations
s = "Hello"
length = len(s)  # 5

# Access characters
first_char = s[0]   # 'H'
second_char = s[1]  # 'e'
last_char = s[4]    # 'o' (index 4 = length-1)

# Invalid access (would cause error):
# error_char = s[5]  # Index 5 is out of bounds (max index is 4)

# Iterate through string
for i in range(len(s)):
    print(s[i])  # Prints H, e, l, l, o

examples.trace:
String: "CAT"
Length: len("CAT") = 3
Valid indices: 0, 1, 2
Access operations:
  s[0] → character at position 0 → 'C'
  s[1] → character at position 1 → 'A'
  s[2] → character at position 2 → 'T'
All accesses are within bounds (0 ≤ index < 3)

examples.input: String: "A", access index 0 and check length
examples.trace:
String: "A"
Length calculation: len("A") = 1
Valid indices: only index 0 (since 0 ≤ index < 1)
Access s[0]: character at position 0 → 'A'
Attempt to access s[1] would be invalid (index out of bounds)

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Add memoization to the brute force recursion to store results of subproblems, avoiding repeated calculations. Suitable for top-down dynamic programming when clarity is prioritized over space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Subsequence with Memoized Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using memoized recursion to find the longest common subsequence between two strings.
ladders.explanation: The longest common subsequence (LCS) problem asks: given two strings, what's the longest sequence of characters that appears in both strings in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguous)? Memoized recursion solves this by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems and caching results to avoid redundant calculations. Intuition: At each position (i,j) in the strings, we have three possibilities: 1) Characters match - we can include this character in our LCS and move to (i-1,j-1). 2) Characters don't match - we need to explore both possibilities of skipping a character from either string. The key insight is that the LCS length at (i,j) depends only on smaller subproblems, making it suitable for memoization. Step-by-step: 1) Define recursive function lcs(i,j) that returns LCS length for prefixes s1[0:i] and s2[0:j]. 2) Base cases: if i==0 or j==0, return 0 (empty prefix). 3) If s1[i-1]==s2[j-1], return 1 + lcs(i-1,j-1). 4) Otherwise, return max(lcs(i-1,j), lcs(i,j-1)). 5) Add memoization table dp[i][j] to store computed results. Time: O(n*m) where n,m are string lengths - each subproblem computed once. Space: O(n*m) for memo table. Connection to L1: This builds on the recursive LCS function by adding memoization. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases, incorrect indexing (0-based vs 1-based), not initializing memo table properly. Real code pattern: def lcs_memo(s1, s2): n,m=len(s1),len(s2); dp=[[-1]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; def solve(i,j): if i==0 or j==0: return 0; if dp[i][j]!=-1: return dp[i][j]; if s1[i-1]==s2[j-1]: dp[i][j]=1+solve(i-1,j-1); else: dp[i][j]=max(solve(i-1,j), solve(i,j-1)); return dp[i][j]; return solve(n,m).

examples.trace: Call solve(5,3). s1[4]=='e', s2[2]=='e' match → dp[5][3]=1+solve(4,2). solve(4,2): s1[3]=='d', s2[1]=='c' no match → dp[4][2]=max(solve(3,2), solve(4,1)). solve(3,2): s1[2]=='c', s2[1]=='c' match → dp[3][2]=1+solve(2,1). solve(2,1): s1[1]=='b', s2[0]=='a' no match → dp[2][1]=max(solve(1,1), solve(2,0)). solve(1,1): s1[0]=='a', s2[0]=='a' match → dp[1][1]=1+solve(0,0)=1+0=1. solve(2,0): base case → 0. So dp[2][1]=max(1,0)=1. Back to solve(3,2): dp[3][2]=1+1=2. solve(4,1): s1[3]=='d', s2[0]=='a' no match → dp[4][1]=max(solve(3,1), solve(4,0)). solve(3,1): s1[2]=='c', s2[0]=='a' no match → dp[3][1]=max(solve(2,1), solve(3,0)). solve(2,1) already computed as 1. solve(3,0): base case → 0. So dp[3][1]=max(1,0)=1. solve(4,0): base case → 0. So dp[4][1]=max(1,0)=1. Back to solve(4,2): dp[4][2]=max(2,1)=2. Back to solve(5,3): dp[5][3]=1+2=3. Final answer: 3.

Call solve(3,3). s1[2]=='c', s2[2]=='f' no match → dp[3][3]=max(solve(2,3), solve(3,2)). solve(2,3): s1[1]=='b', s2[2]=='f' no match → dp[2][3]=max(solve(1,3), solve(2,2)). solve(1,3): s1[0]=='a', s2[2]=='f' no match → dp[1][3]=max(solve(0,3), solve(1,2)). solve(0,3): base case → 0. solve(1,2): s1[0]=='a', s2[1]=='e' no match → dp[1][2]=max(solve(0,2), solve(1,1)). solve(0,2): base case → 0. solve(1,1): s1[0]=='a', s2[0]=='d' no match → dp[1][1]=max(solve(0,1), solve(1,0)). Both base cases → 0. So dp[1][1]=0. Thus dp[1][2]=max(0,0)=0. Thus dp[1][3]=max(0,0)=0. solve(2,2): s1[1]=='b', s2[1]=='e' no match → dp[2][2]=max(solve(1,2), solve(2,1)). solve(1,2) already 0. solve(2,1): s1[1]=='b', s2[0]=='d' no match → dp[2][1]=max(solve(1,1), solve(2,0)). solve(1,1) already 0, solve(2,0) base case → 0. So dp[2][1]=0. Thus dp[2][2]=max(0,0)=0. Back to solve(2,3): dp[2][3]=max(0,0)=0. solve(3,2): s1[2]=='c', s2[1]=='e' no match → dp[3][2]=max(solve(2,2), solve(3,1)). solve(2,2) already 0. solve(3,1): s1[2]=='c', s2[0]=='d' no match → dp[3][1]=max(solve(2,1), solve(3,0)). solve(2,1) already 0, solve(3,0) base case → 0. So dp[3][1]=0. Thus dp[3][2]=max(0,0)=0. Back to solve(3,3): dp[3][3]=max(0,0)=0. Final answer: 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive LCS Function Without Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes LCS length for two string prefixes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the memoized solution: a pure recursive function that computes the LCS length for prefixes of two strings. Intuition: The LCS problem has optimal substructure - the solution can be built from solutions to smaller subproblems. For strings s1 and s2, consider their last characters. If they match, that character must be part of the LCS, so we add 1 to the LCS of the remaining prefixes. If they don't match, the LCS is the maximum of two possibilities: either skip the last character of s1 or skip the last character of s2. This recursive definition naturally breaks the problem down. Step-by-step: 1) Function lcs_rec(i,j) takes indices i and j representing prefixes s1[0:i] and s2[0:j]. 2) Base case: if i==0 or j==0, return 0 (empty string has LCS length 0). 3) If s1[i-1]==s2[j-1], return 1 + lcs_rec(i-1,j-1). 4) Else, return max(lcs_rec(i-1,j), lcs_rec(i,j-1)). Time: O(2^(n+m)) in worst case due to exponential recursive calls. Space: O(n+m) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function becomes efficient when memoized. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indexing, missing base cases, not handling empty strings correctly. Real code pattern: def lcs_rec(s1, s2, i, j): if i==0 or j==0: return 0; if s1[i-1]==s2[j-1]: return 1+lcs_rec(s1,s2,i-1,j-1); else: return max(lcs_rec(s1,s2,i-1,j), lcs_rec(s1,s2,i,j-1)).

examples.trace: lcs_rec(2,2): s1[1]=='b', s2[1]=='c' no match → max(lcs_rec(1,2), lcs_rec(2,1)). lcs_rec(1,2): s1[0]=='a', s2[1]=='c' no match → max(lcs_rec(0,2), lcs_rec(1,1)). lcs_rec(0,2): base case → 0. lcs_rec(1,1): s1[0]=='a', s2[0]=='a' match → 1+lcs_rec(0,0)=1+0=1. So lcs_rec(1,2)=max(0,1)=1. lcs_rec(2,1): s1[1]=='b', s2[0]=='a' no match → max(lcs_rec(1,1), lcs_rec(2,0)). lcs_rec(1,1) already 1. lcs_rec(2,0): base case → 0. So lcs_rec(2,1)=max(1,0)=1. Back to lcs_rec(2,2): max(1,1)=1.
lcs_rec(1,1): s1[0]=='a', s2[0]=='b' no match → max(lcs_rec(0,1), lcs_rec(1,0)). lcs_rec(0,1): base case → 0. lcs_rec(1,0): base case → 0. So max(0,0)=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure of LCS
ladders.desc: Understand the recursive relationship that defines LCS length.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the optimal substructure property of LCS: the solution to the main problem can be constructed from solutions to smaller subproblems. Intuition: Consider two strings X and Y. Let LCS(i,j) be the length of LCS for prefixes X[0:i] and Y[0:j]. The relationship is: 1) If the last characters match (X[i-1]==Y[j-1]), then this character must be included in the LCS, so LCS(i,j)=1+LCS(i-1,j-1). 2) If they don't match, then the LCS is the maximum of two possibilities: either we skip the last character of X (LCS(i-1,j)) or skip the last character of Y (LCS(i,j-1)). This works because the LCS must be in order but not necessarily contiguous. Step-by-step: Formally: LCS(i,j)=0 if i==0 or j==0; LCS(i,j)=1+LCS(i-1,j-1) if X[i-1]==Y[j-1]; else LCS(i,j)=max(LCS(i-1,j), LCS(i,j-1)). This is the fundamental recurrence relation. Time/space: Conceptual - no implementation yet. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (subsequence doesn't require contiguity), misunderstanding the max operation when characters don't match. Real code pattern: This is a conceptual pattern: if match: include character; else: try both skip options and take max.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Prefix Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare characters at specific positions in two strings and handle base cases.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves comparing characters from two strings at given indices and determining the action based on whether they match. Intuition: In LCS, we work with prefixes of strings, so we need to compare the last characters of these prefixes. If they match, we can include that character in the subsequence. If not, we need to consider alternatives. This requires careful indexing since strings are 0-indexed but we're working with prefix lengths. Step-by-step: Given strings s1 and s2, and indices i and j representing prefix lengths (i characters from s1, j from s2): 1) Check if i==0 or j==0 → return 0 (base case). 2) Otherwise, compare s1[i-1] and s2[j-1] (since i-1 is the last index in the prefix). 3) If equal, we have a match. 4) If not equal, we need to explore two scenarios. Time: O(1) per comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This comparison implements the conditions in the optimal substructure. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (using i instead of i-1), forgetting base cases, not understanding that i and j represent lengths not indices. Real code pattern: if i==0 or j==0: return 0; if s1[i-1]==s2[j-1]: # match case; else: # no match case.

examples.trace: i=3, j=2. i!=0 and j!=0. Compare s1[2]=='c' and s2[1]=='c'. They are equal, so it's a match.
i=2, j=2. i!=0 and j!=0. Compare s1[1]=='b' and s2[1]=='d'. They are not equal, so no match.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing String Characters by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a character from a string using its index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing a specific character in a string given its position (index). Intuition: Strings are sequences of characters stored in order. Each character has an index starting from 0 for the first character. Accessing by index allows us to examine individual characters for comparison. Step-by-step: For a string s and an integer index i (0 ≤ i < len(s)), the character at position i is s[i]. If i is out of bounds (i < 0 or i ≥ len(s)), it causes an error. In LCS, we typically access s[i-1] when i represents a prefix length to get the last character. Time: O(1) for direct access. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to get characters for comparison. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing when Python uses 0-based, accessing beyond string length, not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: char = s[index] where index is a valid integer.

examples.trace: String "hello" has indices: 0:'h', 1:'e', 2:'l', 3:'l', 4:'o'. Accessing index 1 returns 'e'.
String "abc" has indices: 0:'a', 1:'b', 2:'c'. Accessing index 0 returns 'a'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a subsequence of a string.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. Intuition: Think of a string as a sequence of characters. A subsequence is any selection of these characters that maintains their original relative order. For example, from "abc", possible subsequences include "", "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc". Key properties: 1) Order is preserved: in "abc", "ac" is a subsequence but "ca" is not. 2) Not necessarily contiguous: "ac" is a subsequence of "abc" even though 'b' is skipped. 3) Every string is a subsequence of itself. 4) The empty string is a subsequence of any string. This is fundamental to LCS because we're looking for the longest sequence common to two strings while preserving order. Step-by-step: Given string s, a subsequence can be formed by choosing any subset of indices i1 < i2 < ... < ik and taking characters s[i1], s[i2], ..., s[ik]. Time/space: Conceptual. Connection to L4: Subsequences are built from individual characters accessed by index. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which requires contiguity), thinking subsequence must include all characters, not understanding empty subsequence. Real code pattern: Conceptual - no code, but understanding that subsequence means maintaining order while allowing deletions.

examples.trace: String "abc" has characters: 'a' at index 0, 'b' at index 1, 'c' at index 2. To form "ac", we take 'a' (index 0) and 'c' (index 2). Since 0 < 2, order is preserved. We skipped 'b' at index 1, which is allowed. So "ac" is a subsequence.
String "abc" has characters in order: 'a','b','c'. To form "ba", we would need 'b' before 'a'. But in the original string, 'a' comes before 'b'. We cannot reverse order, so "ba" is not a subsequence.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Build a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] stores LCS length for prefixes of the strings, filling iteratively based on character matches. This is the standard bottom-up solution with optimal time but higher space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Subsequence with 2D DP Table
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of LCS using bottom-up dynamic programming with O(n*m) time and space.
ladders.explanation: The Longest Common Subsequence problem asks: given two strings text1 and text2, what's the longest sequence of characters that appears in both strings in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguous)? The dynamic programming approach builds a 2D table where dp[i][j] represents the LCS length for the first i characters of text1 and first j characters of text2. The intuition is that we can solve larger problems by combining solutions to smaller subproblems. If characters match (text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]), we can extend the LCS from the previous prefixes. If they don't match, we take the maximum LCS from either excluding the current character from text1 or text2. We fill the table row by row, bottom-up, starting with base cases where either string is empty (LCS length = 0). The final answer is dp[n][m] where n and m are string lengths. This approach guarantees optimality because it explores all possible subsequence combinations through overlapping subproblems. Time complexity is O(n*m) as we fill an n+1 by m+1 table. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the table storage. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (note dp has dimensions n+1 x m+1 but strings are 0-indexed), forgetting base cases, or incorrectly handling the case when characters don't match. The solution connects to the broader DP pattern of solving sequence alignment problems.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Filling the DP Table Cell
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence relation that computes dp[i][j] from previously computed values.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the LCS algorithm: computing one cell dp[i][j] in the dynamic programming table. The cell represents the LCS length for prefixes text1[0:i] and text2[0:j] (first i characters of text1, first j of text2). The intuition is based on considering the last characters of these prefixes. If text1[i-1] equals text2[j-1], this character can be included in the LCS, so we add 1 to the LCS of the remaining prefixes (dp[i-1][j-1]). If they don't match, we have two options: exclude text1[i-1] (look at dp[i-1][j]) or exclude text2[j-1] (look at dp[i][j-1]), and take the maximum. This recurrence captures all possibilities. The algorithm: 1. Check if i==0 or j==0 → return 0 (base case, empty string). 2. If text1[i-1] == text2[j-1], return dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. 3. Else, return max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Time complexity for computing one cell is O(1). Space is O(1) excluding input storage. This feeds into the full problem by being the inner loop computation when filling the entire table. Common mistakes: using wrong indices (remember strings are 0-indexed but dp indices represent lengths), forgetting base cases, or incorrectly implementing the max operation. The pattern appears in many DP problems involving sequences.

examples.trace:
Given: text1="abc", text2="ac", i=2 (prefix "ab"), j=2 (prefix "ac")
Check characters: text1[1]='b', text2[1]='c' → 'b'≠'c'
Since characters don't match, take max of:
- dp[i-1][j] = dp[1][2] = LCS("a", "ac") = 1
- dp[i][j-1] = dp[2][1] = LCS("ab", "a") = 1
max(1,1)=1
But wait, this is incorrect! Let's recompute properly:
i=2 means first 2 chars of text1: "ab"
j=2 means first 2 chars of text2: "ac"
text1[i-1]=text1[1]='b'
text2[j-1]=text2[1]='c'
'b'≠'c' → take max(dp[1][2], dp[2][1])
dp[1][2]=LCS("a","ac")=1 (common 'a')
dp[2][1]=LCS("ab","a")=1 (common 'a')
max(1,1)=1
Actually correct output is 1, not 2. Let me fix the example:
Input: text1="abc", text2="ac", i=3, j=2, dp[i-1][j-1]=1, dp[i-1][j]=1, dp[i][j-1]=2
i=3 → prefix "abc", j=2 → prefix "ac"
text1[2]='c', text2[1]='c' → match!
Return dp[i-1][j-1]+1 = dp[2][1]+1 = LCS("ab","a")+1 = 1+1=2 ✓

Given: text1="xyz", text2="abc", i=1 (prefix "x"), j=1 (prefix "a")
Check characters: text1[0]='x', text2[0]='a' → 'x'≠'a'
Since characters don't match, take max of:
- dp[i-1][j] = dp[0][1] = LCS("", "a") = 0
- dp[i][j-1] = dp[1][0] = LCS("x", "") = 0
max(0,0)=0

testCases.input: text1 = "abcd", text2 = "abed", i=4, j=4, dp[i-1][j-1]=2, dp[i-1][j]=3, dp[i][j-1]=3
text1 = "abcde", text2 = "ace", i=5, j=3, dp[i-1][j-1]=2, dp[i-1][j]=2, dp[i][j-1]=3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for LCS
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical recurrence that defines LCS length.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for solving LCS efficiently is the recurrence relation that expresses the solution in terms of smaller subproblems. Let LCS(i,j) be the length of LCS for the first i characters of text1 and first j characters of text2. The recurrence is: 1. If i=0 or j=0: LCS(i,j)=0 (base case, empty string). 2. If text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]: LCS(i,j) = LCS(i-1,j-1) + 1. 3. Else: LCS(i,j) = max(LCS(i-1,j), LCS(i,j-1)). Intuition: When last characters match, they must be part of the optimal LCS (we can prove by contradiction), so we add 1 to the LCS of the remaining strings. When they don't match, at least one of them is not in the LCS, so we try excluding each and take the better result. This recurrence has optimal substructure (optimal solution can be constructed from optimal solutions of subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (same subproblems computed repeatedly), making it ideal for dynamic programming. The recurrence alone would lead to exponential time if implemented recursively without memoization, but it defines the problem structure. Understanding this recurrence is crucial because it reveals why DP works: we're essentially computing all LCS(i,j) values in increasing order of i and j. This feeds into the table-filling subroutine by providing the formula for each cell. Common mistakes: confusing the indices (i and j represent lengths, not indices), misunderstanding why we take max when characters don't match, or not seeing why the recurrence guarantees optimality.

examples.trace:
Compute LCS(2,2) for first 2 chars of each:
text1[1]='b', text2[1]='c' → 'b'≠'c'
So LCS(2,2)=max(LCS(1,2), LCS(2,1))
First compute LCS(1,2): i=1,j=2
text1[0]='a', text2[1]='c' → 'a'≠'c'
LCS(1,2)=max(LCS(0,2), LCS(1,1))
LCS(0,2)=0 (base case)
LCS(1,1): text1[0]='a', text2[0]='a' match → LCS(1,1)=LCS(0,0)+1=0+1=1
So max(0,1)=1 → LCS(1,2)=1
Now compute LCS(2,1): i=2,j=1
text1[1]='b', text2[0]='a' → 'b'≠'a'
LCS(2,1)=max(LCS(1,1), LCS(2,0))
LCS(1,1)=1 (computed above)
LCS(2,0)=0 (base case)
max(1,0)=1 → LCS(2,1)=1
Back to LCS(2,2)=max(LCS(1,2)=1, LCS(2,1)=1)=1

Compute LCS(1,1) for first char of each:
text1[0]='a', text2[0]='a' → match!
So LCS(1,1)=LCS(0,0)+1
LCS(0,0)=0 (base case, both empty)
0+1=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Characters and Taking Maximum
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to compare string characters and choose the maximum of two values.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two fundamental operations: 1) comparing characters from two strings at specific positions, and 2) selecting the maximum of two numbers. In LCS, we need to check if text1[i] equals text2[j] (character comparison). If they match, we follow one path; if not, we take the maximum of two alternative values. The intuition is simple: character comparison tells us whether the current characters can be paired in the subsequence. Taking the maximum implements the optimal choice when characters don't match—we want the longer subsequence. The algorithm: 1. Access characters text1[i] and text2[j] (ensure indices are valid). 2. Compare using == operator. 3. If true, perform one action (e.g., return x+1). 4. If false, compute max(a,b) and return it. Time complexity is O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into the recurrence relation as the decision logic at each step. Common mistakes: comparing wrong indices, not handling edge cases (empty strings), or incorrectly implementing max (e.g., using min instead). This pattern appears in many string comparison algorithms.

examples.input: text1 = "abc", text2 = "ac", i=0, j=0, match_val=1, no_match_val1=0, no_match_val2=0
examples.trace:
Given: text1="abc", text2="ac", i=0, j=0
Access characters: text1[0]='a', text2[0]='a'
Compare: 'a'=='a' → true
Since match, return match_val=1

examples.input: text1 = "abc", text2 = "def", i=0, j=0, match_val=1, no_match_val1=0, no_match_val2=0
examples.trace:
Given: text1="abc", text2="def", i=0, j=0
Access characters: text1[0]='a', text2[0]='d'
Compare: 'a'=='d' → false
Since no match, take max(no_match_val1=0, no_match_val2=0)
max(0,0)=0

testCases.input: text1 = "a", text2 = "a", i=0, j=0, match_val=5, no_match_val1=3, no_match_val2=4
text1 = "a", text2 = "b", i=0, j=0, match_val=5, no_match_val1=3, no_match_val2=4
text1 = "ab", text2 = "ac", i=1, j=1, match_val=2, no_match_val1=1, no_match_val2=1
text1 = "", text2 = "a", i=0, j=0, match_val=1, no_match_val1=0, no_match_val2=0
text1 = "abc", text2 = "def", i=2, j=2, match_val=3, no_match_val1=2, no_match_val2=2
text1 = "hello", text2 = "world", i=1, j=1, match_val=2, no_match_val1=1, no_match_val2=1
text1 = "same", text2 = "same", i=3, j=3, match_val=4, no_match_val1=3, no_match_val2=3
text1 = "x", text2 = "x", i=0, j=0, match_val=10, no_match_val1=5, no_match_val2=5
text1 = "cat", text2 = "dog", i=0, j=0, match_val=1, no_match_val1=0, no_match_val2=0
text1 = "123", text2 = "124", i=2, j=2, match_val=3, no_match_val1=2, no_match_val2=2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements and Simple Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Retrieve elements from arrays/strings and perform addition/max operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves basic array/string indexing and arithmetic. For LCS, we need to: 1) Access characters from strings using indices (e.g., text1[i] gets the character at position i). 2) Perform addition (e.g., adding 1 to a value when characters match). 3) Compute maximum of two numbers (e.g., max(a,b)). The intuition is that these are the primitive operations that build up the algorithm. String indexing allows us to examine specific characters; addition increments counts; max selects between alternatives. The algorithm: 1. Ensure index is within bounds (0 ≤ i < length). 2. Use array[index] syntax to access element. 3. Use + operator for addition. 4. Use comparison operators (>, <) or max() function to find maximum. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into character comparison and value selection. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, or incorrect arithmetic order. These operations are ubiquitous in programming.

examples.trace:
Given: text="hello", index=1, value=3
Access character: text[1] → second character (0-based) is 'e'
Perform addition: value+1 = 3+1 = 4
Return both: character 'e' and new value 4

Given: a=5, b=7
Compute max: compare 5 and 7
7 > 5, so max=7

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Arrays as Indexed Sequences
ladders.desc: Understand that strings and arrays are ordered collections accessible by position.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces strings and arrays as indexed sequences. A string is a sequence of characters (like "hello"), and an array is a sequence of elements (like [1,2,3]). Both are ordered: each element has a position called an index, starting from 0 (0-based indexing). For example, in string "cat", index 0 is 'c', index 1 is 'a', index 2 is 't'. We can access elements using square brackets: text[0] gives 'c'. The length tells how many elements: len("cat")=3. Indices must be between 0 and length-1; going outside causes errors. The intuition is that many algorithms process sequences by examining elements one by one using indices. This is essential for LCS because we need to compare characters at specific positions in two strings. Time to access an element is O(1). Space depends on the sequence size. This feeds into array indexing operations. Common mistakes: thinking indices start at 1, forgetting that strings are immutable (can't change characters directly), or not checking bounds. Beginners should practice accessing elements and understanding order.

examples.trace:
String "apple" has 5 characters
Index 0: first character → 'a'
Index 1: second character → 'p'
Index 2: third character → 'p'
Index 3: fourth character → 'l'
Index 4: fifth character → 'e'
Length = 5
Valid indices: 0 through 4

Array [10,20,30] has 3 elements
Index 0: first element → 10
Index 1: second element → 20
Index 2: third element → 30
Length = 3
Valid indices: 0 through 2

approaches.name: Space-Optimized DP O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Reduce space usage by keeping only two rows of the DP table, since each cell depends only on previous rows. Ideal for large inputs where memory is a constraint.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized DP Solution for LCS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of longest common subsequence using two-row DP table optimization
ladders.explanation: The space-optimized DP approach solves the classic LCS problem while minimizing memory usage. The intuition is that in the standard DP table dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[0:i] and text2[0:j], each cell depends only on three values: dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1]. This means we only need the current row and previous row at any time, not the entire table. By maintaining just two rows and reusing them, we reduce space from O(n*m) to O(min(n,m)). The algorithm works by iterating through both strings, comparing characters. If they match, we take the diagonal value plus one. If they don't match, we take the maximum of the left and top values. We swap rows after processing each character of the longer string. Time complexity remains O(n*m) since we still examine all character pairs. Space is O(min(n,m)) because we store only two rows of the shorter string's length. This approach is crucial for large inputs where memory constraints matter. Common mistakes include incorrect row swapping logic, off-by-one errors in indices, and forgetting to handle empty strings. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the row management technique as its core optimization.

examples.trace:
Initialize: n=5, m=3. Use text2 as shorter (m=3). Create prev=[0,0,0,0], curr=[0,0,0,0] (length m+1).
Iterate i=1 (text1[0]='a'):
  j=1: text2[0]='a', match → curr[1]=prev[0]+1=0+1=1
  j=2: text2[1]='c', no match → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=0, curr[1]=1)=1
  j=3: text2[2]='e', no match → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=0, curr[2]=1)=1
  Swap: prev=[0,1,1,1], curr=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=2 (text1[1]='b'):
  j=1: 'b' vs 'a' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=1, curr[0]=0)=1
  j=2: 'b' vs 'c' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=1, curr[1]=1)=1
  j=3: 'b' vs 'e' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=1, curr[2]=1)=1
  Swap: prev=[0,1,1,1], curr=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=3 (text1[2]='c'):
  j=1: 'c' vs 'a' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=1, curr[0]=0)=1
  j=2: 'c' vs 'c', match → curr[2]=prev[1]+1=1+1=2
  j=3: 'c' vs 'e' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=1, curr[2]=2)=2
  Swap: prev=[0,1,2,2], curr=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=4 (text1[3]='d'):
  j=1: 'd' vs 'a' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=1, curr[0]=0)=1
  j=2: 'd' vs 'c' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=2, curr[1]=1)=2
  j=3: 'd' vs 'e' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=2, curr[2]=2)=2
  Swap: prev=[0,1,2,2], curr=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=5 (text1[4]='e'):
  j=1: 'e' vs 'a' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=1, curr[0]=0)=1
  j=2: 'e' vs 'c' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=2, curr[1]=1)=2
  j=3: 'e' vs 'e', match → curr[3]=prev[2]+1=2+1=3
Result: prev[3]=3 after final swap (or curr[3]=3).

Initialize: n=3, m=3. Create prev=[0,0,0,0], curr=[0,0,0,0].
Iterate i=1 (text1[0]='a'):
  j=1: vs 'd' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=0, curr[0]=0)=0
  j=2: vs 'e' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=0, curr[1]=0)=0
  j=3: vs 'f' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=0, curr[2]=0)=0
  Swap: prev=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=2 (text1[1]='b'):
  j=1: vs 'd' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=0, curr[0]=0)=0
  j=2: vs 'e' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=0, curr[1]=0)=0
  j=3: vs 'f' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=0, curr[2]=0)=0
  Swap: prev=[0,0,0,0]
Iterate i=3 (text1[2]='c'):
  j=1: vs 'd' → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=0, curr[0]=0)=0
  j=2: vs 'e' → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=0, curr[1]=0)=0
  j=3: vs 'f' → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=0, curr[2]=0)=0
Result: prev[3]=0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Row DP Table Management
ladders.desc: Implement the row swapping mechanism that enables space optimization
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine manages the two-row DP table that reduces space complexity. The intuition is that we only need to keep track of the current row being computed and the previous row, since each cell dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1]. By maintaining two arrays (prev for row i-1, curr for row i) and swapping them after each row completion, we simulate the full DP table without storing it entirely. The algorithm: 1) Initialize prev and curr arrays of length m+1 (where m is shorter string length) with zeros. 2) For each character in the longer string: a) Compute curr[j] for all j using prev and curr values. b) After processing all j, swap prev and curr (so curr becomes new prev, and curr resets for next iteration). 3) Final answer is in prev[m] after all iterations. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(m) per row, contributing to overall O(n*m). Space is O(m) for the two arrays. Common mistakes include forgetting to reset curr values after swap, incorrect swapping logic, and off-by-one errors in array lengths. This feeds into L0 as the core optimization technique that makes the full solution memory-efficient.

examples.input: prev = [0,1,1,1], compute curr for next row with text1_char='c', text2="ace"
examples.trace:
Initialize curr=[0,0,0,0].
For j=1: text2[0]='a' vs 'c' → no match → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=1, curr[0]=0)=1
For j=2: text2[1]='c' vs 'c' → match → curr[2]=prev[1]+1=1+1=2
For j=3: text2[2]='e' vs 'c' → no match → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=1, curr[2]=2)=2
Result: curr=[0,1,2,2]

examples.input: prev = [0,0,0,0], compute curr for next row with text1_char='a', text2="def"
examples.trace:
Initialize curr=[0,0,0,0].
For j=1: text2[0]='d' vs 'a' → no match → curr[1]=max(prev[1]=0, curr[0]=0)=0
For j=2: text2[1]='e' vs 'a' → no match → curr[2]=max(prev[2]=0, curr[1]=0)=0
For j=3: text2[2]='f' vs 'a' → no match → curr[3]=max(prev[3]=0, curr[2]=0)=0
Result: curr=[0,0,0,0]

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Cell Computation Rule
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation that determines each DP cell value based on character comparison
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how to compute each DP cell value dp[i][j] representing LCS of prefixes text1[0:i] and text2[0:j]. The intuition follows from optimal substructure: if characters match, we extend the LCS from shorter prefixes; if they don't match, we take the best LCS excluding one character from either string. The recurrence is: if text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). For i=0 or j=0, dp[i][j]=0 (empty prefix). This works because matching characters must be part of the LCS, so we add 1 to the LCS of remaining prefixes. For mismatches, the LCS either excludes text1[i-1] (using dp[i-1][j]) or text2[j-1] (using dp[i][j-1]). Time per cell is O(1). Space for full table would be O(n*m), but we optimize later. Common mistakes include incorrect indices (off-by-one), misunderstanding the max operation for mismatches, and not handling empty strings. This logic is the heart of L1's row computation and L0's full solution.

examples.trace:
Compare last chars: text1[1]='b', text2[1]='c' → no match.
Take max of dp[i-1][j]=1 (LCS of "a" and "ac") and dp[i][j-1]=1 (LCS of "ab" and "a") = 1.
But wait, this is wrong example. Let's correct: For match case.
Input: text1_prefix="abc", text2_prefix="ac", dp[i-1][j-1]=1, dp[i-1][j]=2, dp[i][j-1]=1.
Compare: text1[2]='c', text2[1]='c' → match.
Then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2.

Compare last chars: text1[1]='b', text2[1]='d' → no match.
Take max of dp[i-1][j]=0 and dp[i][j-1]=0 → 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Prefix Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare characters from two strings and understand prefix relationships
ladders.explanation: This building block involves comparing characters at specific positions in two strings and understanding how prefixes relate. The intuition is that LCS is built incrementally by comparing prefixes: as we extend prefixes character by character, we decide whether to include characters in the subsequence. The algorithm: given strings s1 and s2, indices i and j (1-based for prefixes), compare s1[i-1] and s2[j-1]. If equal, it's a potential match for LCS; if not, we consider excluding one character. This comparison is the fundamental operation that drives the DP recurrence. Time per comparison is O(1). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include using 0-based indices incorrectly, confusing prefixes with substrings, and not handling empty prefixes. This feeds into L2 as the character comparison step that determines which recurrence case to use.

examples.trace:
Prefix s1[0:2]="ab", s2[0:2]="ac". Compare s1[1]='b' and s2[1]='c' → no match.
Wait, i=2 means s1[1]='b', j=2 means s2[1]='c'. So no match.
Let's do match example: s1="abc", s2="acd", i=3, j=2.
Prefix s1[0:3]="abc", s2[0:2]="ac". Compare s1[2]='c' and s2[1]='c' → match.
Prefix s1[0:4]="hell", s2[0:3]="wor". Compare s1[3]='l' and s2[2]='r' → no match.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Update
ladders.desc: Read from and write to arrays using indices, focusing on adjacent cells
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing and updating elements in an array, particularly focusing on how to reference adjacent cells (left, top, diagonal) in a DP table. The intuition is that DP relies on previously computed values stored in arrays; efficient access is crucial. The algorithm: given an array arr representing a row, access arr[j] for current cell, arr[j-1] for left, and in two-row setup, prev[j] for top, prev[j-1] for diagonal. Update arr[j] with new computed value. Time per access/update is O(1). Space is O(n) for array storage. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds (e.g., accessing j-1 when j=0), confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, and incorrect references to adjacent cells. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to store and retrieve prefix comparison results.

examples.trace: For j=2: left is arr[j-1]=arr[1]=1, top is prev[j]=prev[2]=1, diagonal is prev[j-1]=prev[1]=1.
For j=1: left is arr[0]=0, top is prev[1]=0, diagonal is prev[0]=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of subsequences
ladders.explanation: A subsequence is a sequence derived from another sequence by deleting zero or more elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. The intuition is that unlike substrings, subsequences don't need to be contiguous; they preserve relative order but can have gaps. For example, from "abc", subsequences include "", "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc". The algorithm to generate subsequences involves including or excluding each character, leading to 2^n possibilities for length n. Time to list all is exponential, but we only need length for LCS. Space for storage can be large. Common mistakes include confusing subsequences with substrings (which are contiguous), forgetting the empty subsequence, and misunderstanding order preservation. This foundational concept is prerequisite for L4, as LCS builds on comparing subsequences of two strings.

examples.trace:
Start with empty "".
Include/exclude first char 'a': "" and "a".
For each, include/exclude second char 'b':
From "" → "" and "b".
From "a" → "a" and "ab".
Result: "", "a", "b", "ab".

Generate by including/excluding each position:
Position 1: "", "a"
Position 2: From "" → "", "b"; from "a" → "a", "ab"
Position 3: From "" → "", "c"; from "b" → "b", "bc"; from "a" → "a", "ac"; from "ab" → "ab", "abc"
Combine: "", "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc".
